title: Aion Time, Signals & Queries — Live Interaction and Concurrency Mechanics
intention: Define the engine-side mechanisms that let a running workflow process interact with the outside world and coordinate concurrent work: durable timers, signal delivery, read-only queries, child-workflow spawning, and the all/race/map concurrency primitives. A workflow process sleeps for a month and wakes; an external system signals an awaiting workflow and the workflow continues in microseconds; an operator queries a live workflow without perturbing it; a parent spawns children and fans work out across the BEAM. These are the interaction surfaces, and they must be both microsecond-fast on the live path and exactly recoverable after a crash. Every interaction that changes what a workflow observed (a timer firing, a signal arriving, a child completing) is recorded as an aion-core Event so that AD's replay returns the same observation without re-waiting; every interaction that observes without changing (a query) is never recorded. The work here is the live half of durable execution — recording the observable, delivering it over beamr's native mailbox and timer wheel, and recovering it on restart — paired with AD's replay half that reads those same events back.
problem: A workflow may sleep for seconds or for months, and the sleep must survive a VM restart: an in-process timer wheel entry is lost on restart, so a durable record is required, but consulting the store on every short sleep would defeat the BEAM's microsecond latency. The two cannot be served by one mechanism alone.

A signal sent to a workflow must reach the workflow process's mailbox in microseconds during normal execution, yet must also be durable: if the VM restarts after the signal is recorded but before the workflow consumed it, replay must redeliver it without waiting for the external sender to send again.

A query must read a live workflow's state without recording an event, without blocking the workflow's progress, and without any risk of corrupting determinism — a read that accidentally became an event would desynchronise replay forever.

Child workflows, fan-out (all), racing (race), and dynamic fan-out (map) all need to spawn linked BEAM processes, collect their results via selective receive, and propagate cancellation when the workflow process dies or a race loses — and each spawn/result must be recorded so replay reconstructs the same set of children with the same outcomes.

All of these mechanisms sit at the seam between the engine (AE, which owns the workflow process lifecycle and supervision) and durability (AD, which owns event append and replay). The live-delivery and durable-recording mechanics must be defined precisely enough that AE can drive them and AD can replay them, without this cluster owning either lifecycle or replay.

solution.summary: A set of engine-side services within the aion crate, each pairing a live BEAM-native delivery path with a durable aion-core Event. The Timer service is two-tier: short and live timers use beamr's timer wheel; every durable timer is also recorded via EventStore::schedule_timer and recovered on restart and on a periodic tick by polling EventStore::expired_timers, delivering TimerFired to the owning workflow process. Named timers are cancellable (TimerCancelled); anonymous sleeps are engine-assigned TimerIds. The Signal router delivers an external signal to the target workflow process's mailbox for microsecond selective-receive pickup and records SignalReceived so replay redelivers it; if the workflow is not resident it is recorded and delivered on resume. The Query service dispatches a read-only request to the workflow process, which replies via a registered query handler; no event is ever recorded and the workflow's progress is not blocked. The Child-workflow spawner asks AE to start a child execution as a linked process with its own history, recording ChildWorkflowStarted/Completed/Failed; the parent awaits via mailbox (spawn_and_wait) or fires and forgets. The concurrency primitives all/race/map each spawn N linked child processes (activities or child workflows), collect via selective receive, and on race cancel the losers; all spawns and results flow through the same recorded-event discipline. Every recorded interaction is consumed by AD's replay, which returns the recorded observation instead of re-delivering live.

decisions.title: Two-tier timers: live wheel plus durable record
decisions.choice: Every durable timer is recorded in the EventStore (schedule_timer) AND armed on beamr's timer wheel for live firing. The wheel gives microsecond-granularity firing during normal execution with zero store reads; the recorded entry is the recovery source. On the live path the wheel fires first and the recovery poller never sees the timer because it has already fired and been recorded as TimerFired. Rejected: store-polling-only (kills latency for short sleeps) and wheel-only (loses timers across restart).

decisions.title: Timer recovery by expired_timers polling on restart and on tick
decisions.choice: A timer service polls EventStore::expired_timers(now) on engine startup (to fire timers that elapsed while the VM was down) and on a periodic recovery tick (a safety net for timers whose owning process was not resident on the wheel). For each expired entry whose TimerFired has not yet been recorded, it records TimerFired and delivers it to the owning workflow process. The poll interval is engine-configured, never hardcoded. Rejected: relying solely on per-process wheel entries (a non-resident or just-replayed workflow would never re-arm a wheel entry for an already-elapsed timer).

decisions.title: Named, cancellable timers vs anonymous sleeps
decisions.choice: start_timer takes an author-assigned TimerId (named, cancellable via cancel_timer which records TimerCancelled and disarms the wheel entry). sleep is an anonymous timer with an engine-assigned TimerId derived from sequence position; it is not separately cancellable (cancelling a sleep means cancelling the workflow). A cancelled timer that has already fired is a no-op, not an error. Cancellation removes the wheel entry and records TimerCancelled so replay does not deliver a fired event for it.

decisions.title: Signals delivered to the mailbox, recorded for durability
decisions.choice: The signal router records SignalReceived (durably, via append) and delivers the signal to the target workflow process's mailbox so the workflow's selective receive picks it up in microseconds. The record is written before or atomically with delivery so a crash after delivery but before consumption is recoverable. On replay AD returns the recorded SignalReceived without waiting. Rejected: deliver-then-record (a crash between delivery and record loses a consumed-but-unrecorded signal) and record-only-poll (kills live latency).

decisions.title: Signal to a non-resident workflow is recorded and delivered on resume
decisions.choice: If the target workflow process is not currently resident (e.g. suspended awaiting replay, or being restored), the router still records SignalReceived; the signal is delivered to the mailbox once AE makes the process resident, or returned by AD's replay when the workflow re-reaches its receive. The router asks AE to resolve a WorkflowId to a live process handle; it does not itself manage process lifecycle. Rejected: rejecting signals to non-resident workflows (signals must be durable regardless of residency).

decisions.title: Queries never record an event and never block the workflow
decisions.choice: A query is dispatched as a distinct message kind to the workflow process; the process answers it from a registered query handler and replies on a one-shot reply channel. No Event is ever appended for a query. The query is answered at a yield point so it never preempts in-progress deterministic logic mid-step, and it never mutates workflow state. An unknown query name returns a typed QueryError, not a panic. Rejected: recording queries (would desynchronise replay) and reading store-projected state instead of asking the live process (would not reflect in-process state newer than the last appended event).

decisions.title: Query timeout and absent-workflow handling are explicit
decisions.choice: Query dispatch carries an engine-configured timeout; if the workflow process does not reply within it, the service returns QueryError::Timeout rather than hanging. Querying a workflow that is terminal or unknown returns a typed error (NotRunning / Unknown), never a fabricated answer. The query never causes the workflow to be replayed solely to answer it.

decisions.title: Child workflows are linked processes with their own history
decisions.choice: Spawning a child asks AE to start a new workflow execution (distinct WorkflowId/RunId, its own event history) as a process LINKED to the parent process, and records ChildWorkflowStarted in the parent's history. The parent awaits the child result via its mailbox (spawn_and_wait) and on child completion records ChildWorkflowCompleted, or ChildWorkflowFailed on child failure. A fire-and-forget spawn records the start and detaches (monitor rather than blocking await). Cancelling the parent propagates over the link to the child. This cluster owns the spawn-request/result-collection/recording mechanics; AE owns the child's lifecycle and supervision. Rejected: child as an in-parent coroutine (loses independent history and supervision).

decisions.title: all/race/map are selective-receive collectors over linked children
decisions.choice: all spawns N linked child processes and blocks in selective receive until all N results arrive, preserving input order in the result list; any child failure fails the whole all (and cancels the rest). race spawns N, returns the first result to arrive, then cancels the remaining children (kill the linked processes) and records the cancellation. map applies a function to each element of a runtime list to produce the child specs, then behaves like all over those children (dynamic fan-out). All three collect via the workflow process mailbox using selective receive keyed by a per-spawn correlation token. Rejected: a bespoke join runtime — the BEAM mailbox + links already provide fan-out, collection, and cancellation.

decisions.title: Cancellation propagates by killing linked children and recording it
decisions.choice: When the workflow process is cancelled or a race selects a winner, the losing/remaining child processes are terminated via their links (exit signal), and the corresponding cancellation is recorded so replay reconstructs which children were started and which were cancelled rather than completed. The workflow process traps exits so a child exit arrives as a message it can record and act on, not as a fatal signal. Rejected: leaving losers running (resource leak, and replay could observe a late result that live execution discarded).

decisions.title: Correlation tokens match concurrent results to their spawn
decisions.choice: Each spawned child (activity or workflow) in an all/race/map is tagged with a per-spawn correlation token carried in its result message, so selective receive can match an arriving result to the specific spawn position even when many children of the same type run concurrently. The token derives deterministically from the spawning sequence position so replay matches recorded results to spawn positions identically. Rejected: matching by activity/workflow type (ambiguous when the same type is spawned many times in one all/map).

decisions.title: This cluster records live events; AD owns replay of them
decisions.choice: The seam with AD: this cluster defines the LIVE production and durable recording of TimerStarted/Fired/Cancelled, SignalReceived, and ChildWorkflow* events, and the live delivery over mailbox and timer wheel. AD defines REPLAY — how those recorded events are read back so that, on restore, sleep returns immediately for an already-fired timer, receive returns a recorded signal without waiting, and an awaited child returns its recorded result without re-spawning. This cluster does not implement replay; it guarantees the recorded events carry exactly the information replay needs (timer id + fire_at, signal name + payload, child id + result/error, spawn correlation).

constraints.description: unsafe_code = deny across the cluster's modules.
lib.rs/mod.rs are declarations and re-exports only.
Library errors use thiserror domain types; no anyhow in library code; no unwrap/expect in library code; lock poisoning handled explicitly.

These modules live in the aion crate. The seam with AE (workflow process lifecycle, supervision, module loading, residency resolution) is via an explicit engine-facing handle/trait this cluster consumes; this cluster does not manage process lifecycle.

Every interaction that changes what a workflow observed is recorded as an aion-core Event before/at the point of delivery; queries record nothing. No code path delivers a recordable observation without recording it (no silent observation).
Durable timers go through EventStore::schedule_timer / expired_timers; signals and child results go through EventStore append. No bespoke side-channel persistence outside the aion-store contract.

Tests run against aion-store's InMemoryStore; timer expiry, signal delivery + durability, query reply, child spawn, and all/race/map each have explicit test coverage. Time-dependent tests inject the clock — no wall-clock sleeps in tests.
Timer poll intervals, query timeouts, and recovery-tick cadence are engine-configured, never hardcoded defaults (per the no-assumed-defaults rule).

goals: A durable timer service that arms beamr's timer wheel for live firing and records every timer via EventStore::schedule_timer, delivering TimerFired to the owning workflow process when the wheel fires.
Timer recovery that polls EventStore::expired_timers on startup and on a periodic tick, records TimerFired for due timers not yet fired, and delivers them.
Named, cancellable timers (start_timer / cancel_timer recording TimerStarted / TimerCancelled) and anonymous engine-assigned sleeps.

A signal router that records SignalReceived and delivers the signal to the target workflow process's mailbox for microsecond selective-receive pickup, durable across restart and resilient to a non-resident target.

A query service that dispatches a read-only query to a workflow process, returns the handler's reply, records no event, never blocks workflow progress, and handles unknown-query / timeout / not-running explicitly.

A child-workflow spawner that starts a child as a linked process with its own history, records ChildWorkflowStarted/Completed/Failed, supports await (spawn_and_wait) and fire-and-forget, and propagates parent cancellation to the child.

Concurrency primitives all (fan-out, ordered collect), race (first wins, cancel rest), and map (dynamic fan-out) implemented as selective-receive collectors over linked children with per-spawn correlation tokens.
Cancellation that terminates losing/remaining linked children and records the cancellation so replay reconstructs started-vs-cancelled children.

An explicit engine-facing seam (handle/trait) by which this cluster resolves a WorkflowId to a live process, asks AE to spawn a child, and arms/disarms wheel timers — without owning process lifecycle.
Explicit AT/AD seam: recorded events carry exactly what replay needs (timer id + fire_at, signal name + payload, child id + result/error, spawn correlation).
non_goals: No workflow process lifecycle, supervision, module loading, or scheduling — that is AE. This cluster consumes an engine-facing handle for residency and child spawning.
No replay / determinism logic — that is AD. This cluster produces and records the live events; AD reads them back on restore.
No Gleam SDK surface (workflow.receive / sleep / all / race / map / query handler registration) — that is AF. This cluster defines the engine-side mechanisms those bind to via NIFs.
No network transport for how an external signal or query arrives (HTTP/gRPC/WS) — that is AW. This cluster defines the in-engine delivery the network layer calls into.
No activity execution/retry mechanics beyond spawning activity child processes for all/race/map — activity invocation semantics and retry policy live in AE.
No event-store backend — durable timers and event append go through the aion-store contract (libSQL is AS); this cluster uses InMemoryStore in tests.

structure.crates/aion/src/time/timer_service.rs: [AT-002] durable timer service: schedule + wheel arm + TimerFired delivery
structure.crates/aion/src/time/recovery.rs: [AT-003] expired_timers polling on startup + periodic recovery tick
structure.crates/aion/src/signal/router.rs: [AT-005] signal router: record SignalReceived + mailbox delivery
structure.crates/aion/src/signal/resume.rs: [AT-006] non-resident delivery + resume handoff via the engine handle
structure.crates/aion/src/query/service.rs: [AT-007] query dispatch + handler reply + timeout/unknown/not-running handling
structure.crates/aion/src/concurrency/race.rs: [AT-011] race: first wins, cancel + record the rest
structure.crates/aion/src/concurrency/map.rs: [AT-012] map: dynamic fan-out from a runtime list
structure.crates/aion/src/engine_seam.rs: [AT-001] engine-facing handle/trait: residency resolution, child spawn request, wheel arm/disarm
